Question: Care of patients / service users is my organisation's top priority
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to organisational values, patient focus, or concerns that priorities have shifted away from care quality. Look for cues about standards, compassion, or organisational direction. Distinguish from “Workload” (pressure rather than values).

Question: I feel that my role makes a difference to patients / service users
Notes: Use this when comments mention purpose, impact, or meaningful work. Look for cues about contribution, motivation, or feeling disconnected from patient outcomes. Distinguish from “Valued” (recognition).

Question: I would recommend my organisation as a place to work
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to organisational reputation, pride, or willingness to recommend.

Question: If a friend or relative needed treatment I would be happy with the standard of care provided by this organisation
Notes: Use this when comments refer to care quality, safety, or trust in services. Look for cues about standards, patient experience, or concerns about declining quality. Distinguish from “Health & Safety Climate” (staff safety) and “Burnout” (emotional strain).

Question: My organisation acts on concerns raised by patients / service users
Notes: Use this anchor when comments mention patient feedback, complaints handling, or organisational responsiveness. Distinguish from “Raising Concerns” (staff concerns) and “Line Management” (manager behaviour). Use when the focus is on patient?driven issues.

Question: My immediate manager cares about my concerns
Notes: Use this when comments refer to empathy, emotional support, or feeling heard by a manager. Look for cues about compassion, kindness, or dismissiveness. Distinguish from “Line Management” (task?focused behaviour) and “Raising Concerns” (fear of speaking up).

Question: My immediate manager is interested in listening to me when I describe challenges I face
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to being listened to, understood, or taken seriously. Look for cues about communication quality or feeling ignored. Distinguish from “Autonomy & Control” (decision?making) and “Team Working” (peer relationships).

Question: My immediate manager takes effective action to help me with any problems I face
Notes: Use this when comments refer to practical support, problem?solving, or managers failing to act. Look for cues about follow?through, responsiveness, or inaction. Distinguish from “Health & Safety Climate” (systemic issues) and “Workload” (structural pressure).

Question: My immediate manager works together with me to come to an understanding of problems
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to collaborative problem?solving or shared understanding. Look for cues about partnership, empathy, or feeling dismissed. Distinguish from “Team Working” (peer collaboration).

Question: Does your organisation act fairly with regard to career progression/promotion, regardless of e.g. age, disability, ethnic background, gender reassignment, religion, sex, or sexual orientation?
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to fairness, equal opportunities, or bias in promotion. Look for cues about discrimination, favouritism, or blocked progression. Distinguish from “Development” (opportunities) and “Line Management” (manager behaviour unless discrimination is implied).

Question: I think that my organisation respects individual differences (e.g. cultures, working styles, backgrounds, ideas, etc).
Notes: Use this when comments refer to inclusion, respect, or valuing diversity. Look for cues about culture, belonging, or feeling marginalised. Distinguish from “Inclusion” (team?level belonging) and “Compassionate Culture” (organisational values).

Question: In the last 12 months have you personally experienced discrimination at work from manager / team leader or other colleagues?
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to discrimination based on protected characteristics. Look for explicit references to unfair treatment, bias, or exclusion. Distinguish from “Abuse/Bullying” (behaviour not tied to protected characteristics).

Question: In the last 12 months have you personally experienced discrimination at work from patients / service users, their relatives or other members of the public?
Notes: Use this when comments refer to discriminatory behaviour from service users. Look for cues about racism, sexism, or other protected characteristics. Distinguish from “Abuse/Bullying” unless the comment explicitly frames it as discrimination.

Question: I feel a strong personal attachment to my team
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to belonging, team identity, or cohesion. Look for cues about connection, loyalty, or feeling isolated. Distinguish from “Team Working” (function) and “Working Relationships” (interpersonal dynamics).

Question: I feel valued by my team
Notes: Use this when comments refer to appreciation from peers. Look for cues about recognition, support, or feeling overlooked. Distinguish from “Valued” under Recognised & Rewarded (which is broader and includes organisational recognition).

Question: The people I work with are polite and treat each other with respect
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to respect, kindness, or civility. Look for cues about tone, interpersonal behaviour, or team climate. Distinguish from “Abuse/Bullying” (severe behaviour) and “Working Relationships” (role clarity or conflict).

Question: The people I work with are understanding and kind to one another
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to respect, kindness, or civility. Look for cues about tone, interpersonal behaviour, or team climate. Distinguish from “Abuse/Bullying” (severe behaviour) and “Working Relationships” (role clarity or conflict).

Question: My level of pay
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to banding, Agenda for Change, or pay structure. Look for cues about fairness, progression, or mismatch between responsibilities and band. Distinguish from “Cost of Living” (external pressures) and “Valued” (recognition rather than pay).

Question: My level of pay
Notes: Use this when comments refer to affordability, inflation, rising costs, or struggling financially. Look for cues about hardship, financial stress, or pay not keeping pace with living costs. Distinguish from “Banding” (structural pay issues) and “Workload” (pressure unrelated to pay).

Question: My level of pay
Notes: Use this when comments refer to pay but do not specify banding, cost of living, or overtime. This is the default category for general pay dissatisfaction. Distinguish from “Valued” unless the comment explicitly links recognition to pay.

Question: My level of pay
Notes: Use this anchor when comments mention unpaid overtime, staying late, or not being compensated for additional hours. Look for cues about fairness, exploitation, or workload spillover. Distinguish from “Workload” (pressure) unless the comment explicitly links it to unpaid time.

Question: My immediate manager values my work
Notes: Use this anchor when comments explicitly refer to differences in how acute vs non?acute services are valued (e.g., “everything goes to the wards”, “community teams are forgotten”). Look for cues about organisational prioritisation, resource allocation, or visibility of work. Do not infer setting unless the comment makes it clear. This distinction reflects recurring staff perceptions rather than a formal taxonomy. Use the “Other” sub?theme when the comment is about feeling valued but does not specify acute/non?acute context.

Question: The extent to whch organisation values my work
Notes: Use this anchor when comments explicitly refer to differences in how acute vs non?acute services are valued (e.g., “everything goes to the wards”, “community teams are forgotten”). Look for cues about organisational prioritisation, resource allocation, or visibility of work. Do not infer setting unless the comment makes it clear. This distinction reflects recurring staff perceptions rather than a formal taxonomy. Use the “Other” sub?theme when the comment is about feeling valued but does not specify acute/non?acute context.

Look for cues about organisational prioritisation, resource allocation, or visibility of work. Do not infer setting unless the comment makes it clear. This distinction reflects recurring staff perceptions rather than a formal taxonomy. Use the “Other” sub?theme when the comment is about feeling valued but does not specify acute/non?acute context.

Question: The recognition I get for good work
Notes: Use this anchor when comments explicitly refer to differences in how acute vs non?acute services are valued (e.g., “everything goes to the wards”, “community teams are forgotten”). Look for cues about organisational prioritisation, resource allocation, or visibility of work. Do not infer setting unless the comment makes it clear. This distinction reflects recurring staff perceptions rather than a formal taxonomy. Use the “Other” sub?theme when the comment is about feeling valued but does not specify acute/non?acute context.

Question: My immediate manager values my work
Notes: Use this anchor when comments explicitly mention feeling undervalued because of being in a non?clinical or clinical role. Look for cues such as “corporate staff are ignored”, “clinical staff get all the praise”, or “non?clinical roles are invisible”. Avoid assumptions — only apply when staff group is stated or strongly implied. This distinction is based on patterns in staff experience, not an official People Promise structure.

Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to feeling valued or undervalued but do not specify setting or staff group. Look for cues about recognition, appreciation, or feeling overlooked. Distinguish from “Compassionate Leadership” (manager behaviour) and “Team Working” (peer relationships). This is the default category for free?text unless the comment clearly indicates acute/non?acute or clinical/non?clinical distinctions.

Question: I always know what my work responsibilities are
People Promise / Theme: We each have a voice that counts
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to clarity of role, expectations, or confusion about responsibilities. Look for cues about unclear boundaries, mixed messages, or lack of direction. Distinguish from “Line Management” (manager behaviour).

Question: I am trusted to do my job
People Promise / Theme: We each have a voice that counts
Notes: Use this when comments refer to autonomy, micromanagement, or feeling undermined. Look for cues about trust, independence, or unnecessary oversight. Distinguish from “Compassionate Leadership” (empathy) and “Team Working” (peer dynamics).

Question: There are frequent opportunities for me to show initiative in my role
People Promise / Theme: We each have a voice that counts
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to innovation, creativity, or being blocked from taking initiative. Look for cues about bureaucracy, rigid processes, or lack of empowerment. Distinguish from “Development” (career growth).

Question: I am able to make suggestions to improve the work of my team / department
People Promise / Theme: We each have a voice that counts
Notes: Use this when comments refer to ideas being ignored, suggestions not acted on, or barriers to improvement. Look for cues about influence, voice, or organisational openness. Distinguish from “Raising Concerns” (safety or wrongdoing) and “Team Working” (collaboration).

Question: I am involved in deciding on changes introduced that affect my work area / team / department
People Promise / Theme: We each have a voice that counts
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to consultation, involvement in change, or decisions being imposed. Look for cues about top?down change, lack of communication, or feeling excluded. Distinguish from “Senior Management” (strategic decisions) and “Line Management” (local decisions).

Question: I am able to make improvements happen in my area of work
People Promise / Theme: We each have a voice that counts
Notes: Use this when comments refer to empowerment, barriers to change, or lack of authority to act. Look for cues about process constraints, permissions, or organisational blockers. Distinguish from “Resources” (lack of tools) and “Workload” (time pressure).

Question: I have a choice in deciding how to do my work
People Promise / Theme: We each have a voice that counts
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to flexibility, autonomy, or rigid processes. Look for cues about being told exactly how to work, lack of discretion, or overly prescriptive procedures. Distinguish from “Flexible Working” (work?life balance)

Question: I am confident that my organisation would address my concern
People Promise / Theme: We each have a voice that counts
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to organisational follow?through, accountability, or concerns being ignored. Look for cues about distrust, previous negative experiences, or feeling that raising issues is pointless. Distinguish from “Line Management” (local behaviour) unless the comment explicitly refers to organisational processes. Typically routes to People & OD, Governance, or Freedom to Speak Up. Use this when the underlying meaning is about organisational reliability rather than personal safety.

Question: If I spoke up about something that concernd me I am confident my organisation would address my concern
People Promise / Theme: We each have a voice that counts
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to organisational follow?through, accountability, or concerns being ignored. Look for cues about distrust, previous negative experiences, or feeling that raising issues is pointless. Distinguish from “Line Management” (local behaviour) unless the comment explicitly refers to organisational processes. Typically routes to People & OD, Governance, or Freedom to Speak Up. Use this when the underlying meaning is about organisational reliability rather than personal safety.

Question: I would feel secure raising concerns about unsafe clinical practice
People Promise / Theme: We each have a voice that counts
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to psychological safety, fear of repercussions, or reluctance to speak up. Look for emotional cues such as anxiety, intimidation, or fear of blame. Distinguish from “Autonomy & Control” (influence over work) and “Health & Safety Climate” (physical safety). This theme often overlaps with culture, leadership, and bullying — prioritise this sub?theme when the core issue is fear of speaking up. Typically routes to Freedom to Speak Up or senior leadership.

Question: I feel safe to speak up about anything that concerns me in this organisation
People Promise / Theme: We each have a voice that counts
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to psychological safety, fear of repercussions, or reluctance to speak up. Look for emotional cues such as anxiety, intimidation, or fear of blame. Distinguish from “Autonomy & Control” (influence over work) and “Health & Safety Climate” (physical safety). This theme often overlaps with culture, leadership, and bullying — prioritise this sub?theme when the core issue is fear of speaking up. Typically routes to Freedom to Speak Up or senior leadership.

Question: I would feel secure raising concerns about unsafe clinical practice
People Promise / Theme: We each have a voice that counts
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to raising concerns but do not clearly relate to psychological safety or organisational follow?through. Look for cues about uncertainty, mixed experiences, or general dissatisfaction with reporting processes. Use this when the comment is about concerns in general rather than safety or accountability. Distinguish from “Abuse/Bullying” unless the comment explicitly links the issue to speaking up.

Question: My organisation takes positive action on health and wellbeing
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to organisational wellbeing support, initiatives, or lack of action. Look for cues about stress support, wellbeing resources, or feeling unsupported. Distinguish from “Burnout” (emotional exhaustion) and “Illness” (health impacts).

Question: There are enough staff at this organisation for me to do my job properly
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to staffing shortages, unsafe staffing, vacancies, reliance on agency staff, or pressure caused by insufficient numbers. Look for cues about workload, patient safety, or team strain. Distinguish from “Workload” (task pressure) unless the comment explicitly links workload to staffing levels. Typically routes to Workforce Planning or Operational Leadership.

Question: I am able to meet all the conflicting demands on my time at work
Notes: Use this when comments refer to competing priorities, time pressure, or unmanageable workloads. Look for cues about juggling tasks, unrealistic expectations, or constant firefighting. Distinguish from “Stressors” under Morale (emotional impact) and “Staffing Levels” (structural shortages).

Question: I have unrealistic time pressures
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to deadlines, pace of work, or pressure to complete tasks too quickly. Look for cues about rushing, burnout risk, or feeling overwhelmed. Distinguish from “Staffing Levels” unless the comment explicitly links pressure to low staffing.

Question: I am able to meet all the conflicting demands on my time at work
Notes: Use this when comments refer to competing priorities, time pressure, or unmanageable workloads. Look for cues about juggling tasks, unrealistic expectations, or constant firefighting. Distinguish from “Stressors” under Morale (emotional impact) and “Staffing Levels” (structural shortages).

Question: I have adequate materials, supplies and equipment to do my work
Notes: Use this when comments refer to equipment shortages, broken tools, lack of supplies, or poor infrastructure. Look for cues about delays, inefficiency, or safety risks. Distinguish from “Staffing Levels” and “Workload” unless the comment explicitly connects them.

Question: My organisation takes positive action on health and wellbeing
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to organisational wellbeing support, initiatives, or lack of action. Look for cues about stress support, wellbeing resources, or feeling unsupported. Distinguish from “Burnout” (emotional exhaustion) and “Illness” (health impacts).

Question: The last time you experienced physical violence at work, did you or a colleague report it
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to reporting processes, incident handling, or barriers to reporting violence. Look for cues about fear, futility, or lack of follow?up. Distinguish from “Abuse/Bullying” (the experience itself) and “Raising Concerns” (psychological safety).

Question: The last time you experienced harassment, bullying or abuse at work, did you or a colleague report it
Notes: Use this when comments refer to reporting harassment or bullying. Look for cues about confidence in reporting systems, fear of retaliation, or lack of action. Distinguish from “Abuse/Bullying” (the behaviour) and “Safe to Speak Out” (fear of raising issues).

Question: How often, if at all, do you find your work emotionally exhausting?
Notes: Use these anchors when comments refer to emotional exhaustion, burnout, frustration, or depletion. Look for cues about chronic fatigue, dread, or emotional strain. Distinguish from “Workload” (task pressure) and “Illness” (physical health impacts). Prioritise Burnout when the core issue is emotional or psychological exhaustion rather than operational pressure. Typically routes to Wellbeing, OD, or senior leadership.

Question: I can eat nutritious and affordable food while I am working
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to food availability, affordability, or access during shifts. Look for cues about canteen hours, cost, or lack of healthy options. Distinguish from “Cost of Living” unless the comment explicitly links food affordability to pay.

Question: Have you experienced musculoskeletal problems (MSK) as a result of work activities?
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to musculoskeletal pain, injury, or physical strain caused by work. Look for cues about manual handling, equipment, or repetitive strain. Distinguish from “Resources” unless equipment is the cause.

Question: During the last 12 months have you felt unwell as a result of work related stress?
Notes: Use this when comments refer to stress causing physical symptoms. Look for cues about headaches, sleep issues, or stress?related illness. Distinguish from “Burnout” unless the comment emphasises emotional exhaustion.

Question: In the last three months have you ever come to work despite not feeling well enough to perform your duties?
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to presenteeism, pressure to attend work, or guilt about sickness absence. Look for cues about culture, workload, or fear of repercussions. Distinguish from “Burnout” unless emotional exhaustion is the focus.

Notes: Use these anchors when comments refer to violence, harassment, bullying, or sexual misconduct. Look for cues about safety, trauma, fear, or repeated negative behaviour. Question: In the last 12 months, have you had an appraisal, annual review, development review, or Knowledge and Skills Framework (KSF) development review?
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to whether appraisals happened, were missed, or were inconsistent. Look for cues about cancelled reviews, lack of follow?up, or not receiving a review at all. Distinguish from “Development” (opportunities) and “Line Management” (manager behaviour during the review).

Question: It helped me to improve how I do my job
Notes: Use this when comments refer to appraisals being useful, practical, or supportive of performance. Look for cues about feedback quality, clarity, or actionable guidance. Distinguish from “Valued” unless the comment explicitly links feeling valued to the appraisal.

Question: It helped me agree clear objectives for my work
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to goal?setting, clarity of expectations, or lack of clear objectives. Look for cues about direction, alignment, or confusion about priorities. Distinguish from “Autonomy & Control” (decision?making) unless the comment explicitly links objectives to autonomy.

Question: It left me feeling that my work is valued by my organisation
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to feeling valued as a result of the appraisal process. Look for cues about recognition, appreciation, or positive feedback. Distinguish from “Valued” under Recognised & Rewarded unless the comment explicitly ties the feeling to the appraisal experience. This anchor is useful when staff describe appraisals as affirming or demotivating.

Question: This organisation offers me challenging work
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to stretch opportunities, meaningful tasks, or lack of challenge. Look for cues about stagnation, boredom, or wanting more responsibility. Distinguish from “Workload” unless the comment explicitly links challenge to pressure.

Question: There are opportunities for me to develop my career in this organisation
Notes: Use this when comments refer to career progression, blocked pathways, or lack of opportunities. Look for cues about stagnation, limited roles, or unclear progression routes. Distinguish from “Diversity & Equality” unless the comment explicitly links progression barriers to discrimination. Typically routes to OD or Workforce Planning.

Question: I have opportunities to improve me knowledge and skills
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to training availability, skill development, or lack of learning opportunities. Look for cues about access, relevance, or outdated training. Distinguish from “Appraisals” unless the comment explicitly links development to the appraisal process.

Question: I feel supported to develop my potential
Notes: Use this when comments refer to encouragement, support, or barriers to personal growth. Look for cues about manager support, organisational investment, or feeling overlooked. Distinguish from “Valued” unless the comment explicitly links development to recognition.

Question: I am able to access the right learning and development opportunities when I need to
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to access issues, timing, availability, or barriers to training. Look for cues about workload preventing attendance, lack of funding, or training being oversubscribed. Distinguish from “Workload” unless the comment explicitly links workload to inability to attend training.

Question: I am able to access clinical supervision opportunities when I need to
Notes: Use this when comments refer to clinical supervision availability, quality, or frequency. Look for cues about reflective practice, professional support, or lack of protected time. Distinguish from “Line Management” unless the comment explicitly links supervision to managerial behaviour.

Question: The opportunities for flexible working patterns
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to availability, fairness, or inconsistency of flexible working options. Look for cues about shift patterns, remote working, compressed hours, or requests being declined. Distinguish from “Support for Work?Life Balance” unless the comment explicitly links flexibility to personal life pressures. Use this when the focus is on the options themselves, not the impact.

Question: My organisation is committed to helping me balance my work and home life
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to organisational support (or lack of it) for balancing work with personal responsibilities. Look for cues about childcare, caring responsibilities, rigid scheduling, or lack of understanding. Distinguish from “Flexible Working” unless the comment explicitly refers to specific working patterns. Use this when the focus is on organisational commitment rather than specific arrangements.

Question: I achieve a good balance between m work life and my home life
Notes: Use this when comments refer to personal experience of balance or imbalance. Look for cues about exhaustion, spillover, or difficulty switching off. Distinguish from “Burnout” unless the comment emphasises emotional exhaustion rather than time balance.

Question: I can approach my immediate manager to talk openly about flexible working
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to manager openness, supportiveness, or barriers to discussing flexible working. Look for cues about fear of asking, inconsistent treatment, or dismissive responses. Distinguish from “Compassionate Leadership” unless the comment explicitly links the issue to flexible working. This anchor is useful when the comment is about managerial gatekeeping rather than organisational policy.

Question: The team I work in has a set of shared objectives
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to shared goals, alignment, or lack of clarity about team direction. Look for cues about inconsistent priorities, unclear expectations, or teams pulling in different directions. Distinguish from “Line Management” unless the comment explicitly links the issue to managerial communication.

Question: The team I work in often meets to discuss the team's effectiveness
Notes: Use this when comments refer to team meetings, reflective practice, or lack of time to discuss improvement. Look for cues about communication, collaboration, or missed opportunities to review performance. Distinguish from “Autonomy & Control” unless the comment explicitly links meetings to decision?making.

Question: I receive the respect I deserve from colleagues at work
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to respect, civility, or interpersonal treatment within the team. Look for cues about kindness, dismissiveness, or feeling undervalued by peers. Distinguish from “Inclusion” (team belonging) and “Abuse/Bullying” (harmful or targeted behaviour). Use this when the issue is everyday respect rather than severe misconduct.

Question: Team members understand each other's roles
Notes: Use this when comments refer to role clarity within the team, misunderstandings about responsibilities, or friction caused by unclear boundaries. Look for cues about duplication, gaps, or confusion. Distinguish from “Autonomy & Control” unless the comment explicitly links role clarity to decision?making.

Question: I enjoy working with the colleagues in my team
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to enjoyment, camaraderie, or positive team relationships. Look for cues about morale, support, or team spirit. Distinguish from “Motivation” unless the comment explicitly links enjoyment to job enthusiasm.

Question: My team has enough freedom in how to do its work
Notes: Use this when comments refer to team?level autonomy, flexibility, or constraints. Look for cues about rigid processes, lack of discretion, or team?wide restrictions. Distinguish from “Autonomy & Control” (individual autonomy) unless the comment explicitly refers to the team.

Question: In my team disagreements are dealth with constructively
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to conflict resolution, communication, or how disagreements are handled. Look for cues about tension, avoidance, or constructive dialogue. Distinguish from “Working Relationships” unless the comment explicitly refers to conflict rather than general interpersonal dynamics. Use this when the focus is on how conflict is managed, not whether it exists.

Question: Teams within this organisation work well together to achieve their objectives
Notes: Use this when comments refer to cross?team collaboration, silo working, or organisational coordination. Look for cues about barriers between departments, poor communication, or strong collaboration. Distinguish from “Senior Management” unless the comment explicitly links cross?team issues to leadership decisions.

Question: My immediate manager gives me clear feedback on my work
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to feedback quality, clarity, or lack of constructive guidance. Look for cues about communication, expectations, or feeling unsupported. Distinguish from “Appraisals” unless the comment explicitly links feedback to the appraisal process.

Question: My immediate manager asks for my opinion before making decisions that affect my work
Notes: Use this when comments refer to consultation, involvement, or feeling excluded from decisions. Look for cues about top?down decisions, lack of voice, or poor communication. Distinguish from “Autonomy & Control” unless the comment explicitly links consultation to empowerment. Use this when the focus is on managerial behaviour rather than organisational processes.

Question: My immediate manager takes a positive interest in my health and wellbeing
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to manager support for wellbeing, empathy, or lack of care. Look for cues about compassion, check?ins, or dismissiveness. Distinguish from “Health & Safety Climate” unless the comment explicitly links wellbeing to organisational action.

Question: I look forward to going to work
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to enthusiasm, enjoyment, or dread about coming to work. Look for cues about anticipation, morale, or emotional connection to the job. Distinguish from “Burnout” unless the comment emphasises exhaustion rather than motivation.

Question: I am enthusiastic about my job
Notes: Use this when comments refer to passion, interest, or loss of enthusiasm. Look for cues about pride, stagnation, or feeling disconnected. Distinguish from “Valued” unless the comment explicitly links enthusiasm to recognition.

Question: Time passes quickly when I am working
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to flow, engagement, or boredom. Look for cues about meaningful work, monotony, or feeling disengaged. Distinguish from “Workload” unless the comment explicitly links time pressure to task volume.

Question: There are frequent opportunities for me to show initiative in my role
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to innovation, creativity, or being blocked from taking initiative. Look for cues about bureaucracy, rigid processes, or lack of empowerment. Distinguish from “Development” (career growth) and “Involvement” under Staff Engagement (participation rather than autonomy).

Question: I am able to make suggestions to improve the work of my team / department
Notes: Use this when comments refer to ideas being ignored, suggestions not acted on, or barriers to improvement. Look for cues about influence, voice, or organisational openness. Distinguish from “Raising Concerns” (safety or wrongdoing) and “Team Working” (collaboration).

Question: I am able to make improvements happen in my area of work
Notes: Use this when comments refer to empowerment, barriers to change, or lack of authority to act. Look for cues about process constraints, permissions, or organisational blockers. Distinguish from “Resources” (lack of tools) and “Workload” (time pressure).

Question: Care of patients / service users is my organisation's top priority
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to organisational values, patient focus, or concerns that priorities have shifted away from care quality. Look for cues about standards, compassion, or organisational direction. Distinguish from “Advocacy” (which is about recommending the organisation) and “Workload” (pressure rather than values).

Question: I would recommend my organisation as a place to work
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to organisational reputation, pride, or willingness to recommend. Distinguish from “Advocacy” under Staff Engagement (which is similar but more sentiment?driven). Use this when the comment is about organisational culture rather than personal experience.

Question: If a friend or relative needed treatment I would be happy with the standard of care provided by this organisation
Notes: Use this when comments refer to care quality, safety, or trust in services. Look for cues about standards, patient experience, or concerns about declining quality. Distinguish from “Health & Safety Climate” (staff safety) and “Burnout” (emotional strain).

Question: I often think about leaving this organisation
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to wanting to leave, considering other jobs, or feeling pushed out. Look for cues about dissatisfaction, burnout, conflict, or lack of progression. Distinguish from “Motivation” unless the comment explicitly links motivation to leaving. Typically routes to senior leadership or Workforce Planning.

Question: I will probably look for a job at a new organisation in the next 12 months
Notes: Use this when comments refer to active job searching or planning to leave. Look for cues about intent, timelines, or reasons for wanting to move on. Distinguish from “Work Pressure” unless the comment explicitly links leaving to workload.

Question: As soon as I can find another job, I will leave this organisation
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to current job applications, interviews, or imminent departure. Look for cues about urgency, frustration, or decisive action. Distinguish from “Thinking About Leaving” unless the comment explicitly refers to active steps.

Question: I am able to meet all the conflicting demands on my time at work
Notes: Use this when comments refer to competing priorities, time pressure, or unmanageable workloads. Look for cues about juggling tasks, unrealistic expectations, or constant firefighting. Distinguish from “Stressors” under Morale (emotional impact) and “Staffing Levels” (structural shortages).

Question: I have adequate materials, supplies and equipment to do my work
Notes: Use this when comments refer to equipment shortages, broken tools, lack of supplies, or poor infrastructure. Look for cues about delays, inefficiency, or safety risks. Distinguish from “Staffing Levels” and “Workload” unless the comment explicitly connects them.

Question: There are enough staff at this organisation for me to do my job properly
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to staffing shortages, unsafe staffing, vacancies, reliance on agency staff, or pressure caused by insufficient numbers. Look for cues about workload, patient safety, or team strain. Distinguish from “Workload” (task pressure) unless the comment explicitly links workload to staffing levels. Typically routes to Workforce Planning or Operational Leadership.

Question: I always know what my work responsibilities are
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to clarity of role, expectations, or confusion about responsibilities. Look for cues about unclear boundaries, mixed messages, or lack of direction. Distinguish from “Stressors” under Morale (emotional impact) and “Line Management” (manager behaviour).

Question: I am involved in deciding on changes introduced that affect my work area / team / department
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to consultation, involvement in change, or decisions being imposed. Look for cues about top?down change, lack of communication, or feeling excluded. Distinguish from “Senior Management” (strategic decisions) and “Line Management” (local decisions).

Question: I have unrealistic time pressures
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to deadlines, pace of work, or pressure to complete tasks too quickly. Look for cues about rushing, burnout risk, or feeling overwhelmed. Distinguish from “Staffing Levels” unless the comment explicitly links pressure to low staffing.

Question: I have a choice in deciding how to do my work
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to flexibility, autonomy, or rigid processes. Look for cues about being told exactly how to work, lack of discretion, or overly prescriptive procedures. Distinguish from “Flexible Working” (work?life balance) and “Stressors” (emotional impact).

Question: Relationships at work are strained
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to tension, conflict, or deteriorating relationships within the workplace. Look for cues about strained communication, unresolved disagreements, or a negative team atmosphere. Distinguish from “Working Relationships” (role clarity or collaboration) and from “Abuse/Bullying” (severe or targeted behaviour). Use this when the emotional impact on morale is the primary focus.

Question: I receive the respect I deserve from my colleagues at work
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to civility, respect, or feeling undervalued by peers. Look for cues about tone, courtesy, or interpersonal treatment. Distinguish from “Inclusion” (belonging and team identity) and from “Abuse/Bullying” (harmful or hostile behaviour). Use this when the issue is everyday respect rather than severe misconduct.

Question: My immediate manager encourages me at work
Notes: Use this anchor when comments refer to motivation, encouragement, or emotional support from a manager. Look for cues about praise, reassurance, or feeling uplifted. Distinguish from “Compassionate Leadership” (empathy and listening) and “Line Management” (task?focused behaviour). Use this when the focus is on morale?boosting encouragement rather than practical support.

Use this anchor when comments refer to COVID?19 impacts that do not fit into existing themes. Look for cues about pandemic?related workload, long?term effects, service disruption, or organisational response. Distinguish from “Workload” (general pressure), “Health & Safety Climate” (safety processes), and “Burnout” (emotional exhaustion). Use this when COVID is the explicit context or cause.

Use this anchor when comments refer to physical or digital infrastructure issues. Look for cues about buildings, estates, IT systems, equipment failures, or environmental conditions. Distinguish from “Resources” (tools needed for work) unless the comment explicitly refers to wider infrastructure problems. Use this when the focus is on organisational systems or physical environment rather than personal workload.

Use this anchor when comments refer to strategic decisions made by senior leaders. Look for cues about direction, priorities, restructuring, or high?level choices. Distinguish from “Line Management” (local decisions) and “Autonomy & Control” (individual influence). Use this when the comment concerns organisation?wide decision?making.

Use this anchor when comments refer to visibility, communication, or engagement from senior leaders. Look for cues about leaders being distant, absent, or disconnected from staff. Distinguish from “Team Working” (peer engagement) and “Compassionate Leadership” (manager empathy). Use this when the focus is on senior leadership presence and communication.

Use this anchor when comments refer to rigid hierarchy, power imbalance, or difficulty accessing senior leaders. Look for cues about layers of management, bureaucracy, or feeling “at the bottom”. Distinguish from “Autonomy & Control” (influence over work) and “Raising Concerns” (psychological safety). Use this when hierarchy itself is the issue.

Use this anchor when comments refer to senior leadership but do not clearly relate to decision?making, engagement, or hierarchy. Look for general statements about leadership culture, trust, or organisational direction. Distinguish from “Compassionate Culture” unless the comment explicitly refers to senior leaders.

Use this anchor when comments refer to national?level decisions, policies, funding, or political context. Look for cues about NHS England, government policy, national pay decisions, or system?wide pressures. Distinguish from “Senior Management” (local leadership) and “Pay” (personal financial concerns). Use this when the issue is external to the organisation.

Use this anchor when comments do not fit any existing theme or sub?theme despite review. Look for cues that the comment is either too general, too mixed, or outside the scope of the People Promise and Engagement/Morale frameworks. Use sparingly and only after boundary checks. Distinguish from “Redacted/Insufficient” (DQMI exclusion) and from “Multiple Themes” (where coding to two categories is more appropriate).
